questions.questionText: The most common type of lung cancer overall is:
questions.optionB: Adenocarcinoma (most common in non-smokers, women, and overall)
questions.explanation: Adenocarcinoma: most common lung cancer (~40%). Peripheral location. Most common in non-smokers and women (though still strongly associated with smoking). Subtypes: lepidic (formerly BAC), acinar, papillary, solid, micropapillary. Histology: gland formation, mucin production. Molecular: EGFR mutations (respond to TKIs — gefitinib), ALK rearrangements (crizotinib), KRAS mutations. Squamous cell: central, cavitating, keratin pearls. Small cell: central, neuroendocrine, paraneoplastic. Reference: Robbins — Lung Cancer.

questions.optionB: Ferruginous bodies (asbestos bodies — golden-brown, dumbbell-shaped, iron-coated fibers) and interstitial fibrosis
questions.explanation: Asbestosis: diffuse interstitial pulmonary fibrosis from asbestos exposure. Ferruginous bodies (asbestos bodies): translucent asbestos fibers coated with hemosiderin (iron) → golden-brown, beaded, dumbbell-shaped (Prussian blue positive). Lower lobe predominant fibrosis. Complications: pleural plaques (most common manifestation of asbestos exposure — NOT pre-malignant), malignant mesothelioma (pleural — psammoma bodies), bronchogenic carcinoma (risk multiplied by smoking). Reference: Robbins — Asbestosis.

questions.optionB: Non-caseating granulomas, bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, elevated ACE, and hypercalcemia
questions.explanation: Sarcoidosis: systemic granulomatous disease of unknown etiology. Non-caseating epithelioid granulomas in multiple organs (lungs most common). Features: bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (most common radiologic finding), interstitial lung disease, erythema nodosum, uveitis, elevated serum ACE, hypercalcemia (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D from macrophages in granulomas). Asteroid bodies and Schaumann bodies (nonspecific). More common in African Americans, women. Kveim test (historical). Reference: Robbins — Sarcoidosis.

questions.optionB: Permanent enlargement of air spaces distal to terminal bronchioles with destruction of alveolar walls (without fibrosis)
questions.explanation: Emphysema: destructive enlargement of air spaces distal to terminal bronchioles. Types: Centriacinar/centrilobular (most common, upper lobes, SMOKING), Panacinar/panlobular (lower lobes, α1-antitrypsin deficiency), Paraseptal (subpleural, young adults, pneumothorax risk), Irregular. Pathogenesis: protease-antiprotease imbalance (neutrophil elastase vs α1-antitrypsin) + oxidative stress. 'Pink puffer' (tachypneic, thin, barrel chest). PFTs: obstructive pattern, increased TLC, decreased DLCO. Reference: Robbins — Emphysema.

questions.questionText: Pulmonary tuberculosis shows characteristic pathology of:
questions.optionB: Caseating granulomas with Langhans giant cells, Ghon focus/complex
questions.explanation: Pulmonary TB (Mycobacterium tuberculosis): caseating granulomas (central caseous necrosis surrounded by epithelioid cells and Langhans giant cells). Primary TB: Ghon focus (subpleural lesion, usually lower lobe) + ipsilateral hilar lymph node → Ghon complex. Usually heals with calcification (Ranke complex). Secondary (reactivation) TB: apex of upper lobes (high O2 tension). Miliary TB: hematogenous dissemination → tiny granulomas in all organs. Acid-fast bacilli (ZN stain). Reference: Robbins — Pulmonary TB.

questions.questionText: ARDS (acute respiratory distress syndrome) is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Diffuse alveolar damage with hyaline membrane formation, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema
questions.explanation: ARDS: acute onset of severe hypoxemia with bilateral pulmonary infiltrates NOT due to heart failure (non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema). Pathology: diffuse alveolar damage (DAD). Phases: (1) Exudative (first week): hyaline membranes (fibrin + necrotic pneumocytes), edema, capillary congestion. (2) Proliferative (1-3 weeks): type II pneumocyte proliferation, fibroblast organization. Causes: sepsis (most common), trauma, aspiration, pancreatitis, transfusion (TRALI). Mortality ~40%. Reference: Robbins — ARDS.

questions.questionText: Silicosis is caused by inhalation of:
questions.optionB: Crystalline silica (silicon dioxide) → silicotic nodules with concentric layers of collagen
questions.explanation: Silicosis: inhalation of crystalline silica (quartz) — sandblasting, stone cutting, mining. Silicotic nodules: concentrically arranged layers of dense collagen ('whorled'), birefringent silica particles (weakly birefringent under polarized light). Upper lobe predominant. Eggshell calcification of hilar lymph nodes (pathognomonic on CXR). Complications: increased susceptibility to TB (silico-tuberculosis — silica impairs macrophage function), progressive massive fibrosis. Caplan syndrome: silicosis + RA. Reference: Robbins — Silicosis.

questions.questionText: Small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC) is strongly associated with:
questions.optionB: Heavy smoking, central location, neuroendocrine origin, and paraneoplastic syndromes (SIADH, Cushing, Lambert-Eaton)
questions.explanation: SCLC (oat cell carcinoma): ~15% of lung cancers, strongest association with SMOKING. Central location. Neuroendocrine tumor (express chromogranin, synaptophysin, NSE). Extremely aggressive, early metastasis. Paraneoplastic syndromes: SIADH (most common paraneoplastic), ectopic ACTH (Cushing), Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (anti-VGCC antibodies). NOT surgically resectable (usually disseminated at diagnosis). Treatment: chemo-radiation. Reference: Robbins — SCLC.

questions.questionText: Mesothelioma is a malignancy of the pleura strongly associated with:
questions.optionB: Asbestos exposure (long latency period of 25-45 years)
questions.explanation: Malignant mesothelioma: tumor arising from mesothelial cells (pleura > peritoneum). Strongly associated with asbestos exposure (amphibole fibers — crocidolite most dangerous). Long latency period (25-45 years). Histology: epithelioid (most common), sarcomatoid, or biphasic. Immunohistochemistry: calretinin+, CK5/6+, WT-1+, D2-40+. Psammoma bodies may be present. Encases the lung. NOT strongly related to smoking (unlike bronchogenic carcinoma). Very poor prognosis. Reference: Robbins — Mesothelioma.

questions.questionText: Goodpasture syndrome affects the lungs with:
questions.optionB: Pulmonary hemorrhage (hemoptysis) due to anti-GBM antibodies against alveolar basement membrane
questions.explanation: Goodpasture syndrome: anti-GBM antibodies target type IV collagen (α3 chain) in both glomerular and alveolar basement membranes → pulmonary hemorrhage (hemoptysis, dyspnea) + rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Lung biopsy: intra-alveolar hemorrhage, hemosiderin-laden macrophages. IF: linear IgG deposition along basement membrane. Young males, often triggered by smoking or infections. Treatment: plasmapheresis + immunosuppression. Reference: Robbins — Goodpasture Lung.
